era_context: The Roman Empire under Antoninus Pius continues its period of relative peace and stability. The Antonine Wall in Britain is under construction. In China, the Later Han Dynasty is in the early years of Emperor Hui's reign (r. 146–168 CE), with the Liang family in control of the regency. The Parthian Empire under Vologases IV dominates the Middle East. This is a period of consolidation and internal development across the major civilisations.

events.title: Antonine Wall construction begins in Scotland under Lollius Urbicus
events.description: In 147 CE (or possibly 142–143 CE, with scholarly debate on the exact date), the Roman governor Lollius Urbicus orders the construction of the Antonine Wall in Scotland under the direction of Emperor Antoninus Pius. The wall is designed to stretch approximately 37 miles between the Firth of Forth and Firth of Clyde, replacing Hadrian's Wall as the primary frontier of Roman Britain. This represents a northward expansion of Roman military control and a significant engineering and logistical achievement. The wall's construction will take several years to complete, with the project substantially finished by c. 150 CE.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from the Antonine Wall

events.certainty_note: The exact date of the Antonine Wall's construction is debated by scholars. Most sources place it between 142–150 CE, with 142–143 CE being the most commonly cited date. The year 147 CE is used here as a representative point within this range, but the actual construction may have begun earlier or later.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman military expansion under Antoninus Pius

events.title: Later Han Dynasty: Emperor Hui's early reign under Liang family regency
events.description: In 147 CE, the Later Han Dynasty is in the early years of Emperor Hui's reign (r. 146–168 CE). The young emperor is effectively controlled by the Liang family, particularly his mother Empress Dowager Liang and her brother Liang Ji, who holds significant military and political power. This period marks the beginning of a shift in Han governance toward dominance by imperial consort families, moving away from the earlier meritocratic bureaucracy. The Liang family's control is secure in 147 CE, but tensions with eunuch factions are beginning to emerge.

sources.name: Hou Hanshu (History of the Later Han)

events.certainty_note: Emperor Hui's reign dates and the Liang family's dominance are well-documented in Chinese historical records.
cross_references: Part of broader Later Han Dynasty history

events.title: Parthian Empire under Vologases IV maintains control of Mesopotamia and Silk Road
events.description: In 147 CE, the Parthian Empire under King Vologases IV (r. c. 147–191 CE) is at the height of its power, controlling Mesopotamia, the Levant, and the crucial Silk Road trade routes. Vologases IV has recently come to power (c. 147 CE) and is consolidating his rule. The Parthian Empire maintains a balance of power with the Roman Empire, with neither side able to decisively defeat the other. The Parthians' control of the intermediary trade routes gives them significant economic and political leverage.
key_figures: Vologases IV (Parthian King, r. c. 147–191 CE)

sources.name: Roman historical sources (Dio Cassius, later compiled)
Parthian sources (fragmentary, known through later Islamic historians)

events.certainty_note: Vologases IV's reign is dated c. 147–191 CE, placing him on the throne in 147 CE. However, the exact date of his accession is uncertain, and some sources place it slightly earlier or later.

events.title: Silk Road trade networks continue to flourish
events.region: Central Asia, China, Parthia, Roman Empire
events.description: In 147 CE, the Silk Road trade networks connecting East and West continue to operate at high capacity. Chinese goods flow westward to the Roman Empire and Parthia, while Roman and Central Asian goods flow eastward. The Later Han Dynasty maintains diplomatic and trade relations with the Parthian Empire, which controls the crucial intermediary routes. This period represents the height of pre-Islamic Eurasian trade integration.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Silk Road sites

events.certainty_note: Trade networks are confirmed through archaeological evidence.
cross_references: Part of broader economic integration of Eurasia

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Antonine Wall's construction to 147 CE is uncertain. Modern scholarship places the construction between 142–150 CE, with 142–143 CE being more commonly cited. The year 147 CE is used here as a representative point, but the actual construction may have begun earlier. Additionally, the characterization of Vologases IV's reign as beginning in 147 CE is approximate; some sources place his accession slightly earlier or later.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 147 CE is sparse and undistinguished. The year is not marked by any major recorded events in surviving sources. Most of what we know about this year is inferred from broader patterns of the Antonine period in Rome and the Later Han Dynasty in China. The year 147 CE is often subsumed into general narratives of the mid-2nd century CE. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and Southeast Asia remain almost entirely absent from contemporary written sources.

graph_edges.note: Antonine Wall construction in 147 CE leads to completion and operational status by 150 CE
Early Liang family dominance in 147 CE continues through 150 CE
Vologases IV's consolidation of power in 147 CE leads to stable Parthian-Roman frontier by 150 CE
